Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
v
Preface
The Musical Arts Education (MAE) Teaching Module for Form 1 has been developed in 
response to the introduction of the Competence-Based Curriculum (CBC), a transformative 
initiative aimed at enhancing quality education and relevance.
This teaching module is designed to support teachers to effectively deliver Musical Arts Education 
lessons under the new curriculum. Its primary objectives are to, bridge the gap created by the 
absence of approved textbooks and equip teachers with practical skills and assessments tailored 
to learners’ needs and levels.
The Ministry of Education acknowledges the challenges associated with transitioning to a new 
curriculum. Consequently, this module reflects our commitment to providing teachers with 
structured guidance, suggested activities, formative and summative assessments aligned with 
the CBC’s objectives.
While this module serves as a foundational resource, we encourage teachers to supplement it 
with their own research and innovations to address potential gaps during implementation. The 
suggested activities and assessments are designed to foster practical and critical thinking skills 
in learners, ensuring relevance and adaptability across diverse school contexts.
Teachers are encouraged to tailor these suggestions to their unique classroom dynamics and 
explore alternative approaches when necessary. We hope this module will empower teachers 
to navigate the new competence-based curriculum with confidence, inspiring and equipping 
learners with applied knowledge, skills, and values.
Kelvin Mambwe (PhD)
Permanent Secretary- Educational Services
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

Musical Arts Education
Teaching Module
vi
Acknowledgement	
The Musical Arts Education Teaching Module for Form 1 is the culmination of a collaborative 
and inclusive development process. I wish to extend my profound appreciation to individuals, 
institutions, and organisations that contributed to the module’s development, their invaluable 
input and expertise have enriched the content and quality of this teaching module.
I also acknowledge the dedication and hard work of the staff at the Curriculum Development 
Centre, whose tireless efforts ensured the successful completion of the teaching module 
development process.
Charles Ndakala (Dr.)
Director - Curriculum Development
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
vii
Introduction 
The Competence-Based Curriculum (CBC) is designed to equip learners with competences 
necessary to navigate the complexities of the modern world. This Musical Arts Education 
(MAE) teaching module is tailored to support teachers in implementing the CBC, focusing on 
the attainment of specific competences of specified sub-topics as indicated in the syllabus, but 
aligned to the general competences like creativity and innovation, critical thinking, problem-
solving, and effective communication.
Each specific competence is supported by carefully designed learning activities that cater for 
diverse learning styles and abilities. These learning activities further give guidance on how they 
should be effectively administered to the learners to meet the expected standards that speak to the 
specific competences. Furthermore, this module encourages teachers to assess learners’ progress 
using a range of strategies.
By using this MAE teaching module, teachers will be able to support learners to acquire 
competences outlined in the syllabus. This will enable the development of practical skills, 
enhancement of talents, creativity, self-expression and sense of aesthetics among learners.

Musical Arts Education
Teaching Module
viii
Icons used in the module
Summary                          	
Terminology      
     
 Assessment

  Activity

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
1
TOPIC: 1.5 MELODY
Introduction 
This topic is broken down into two subtopics which are: 1.5.1 Melodic motion and 1.5.2 Simple 
melody writing.
What is Melody?
Melody is a fundamental element of music often considered the most memorable part of a song 
or composition. It is the backbone of music, the thread that weaves together pitches, rhythms, 
and emotions to create a rich blend of sound. It is defined as the succession of pitches heard in a 
sequence, creating a musical line or theme. 
Melody   has   practical application in various aspects of life, such as composing   jingles   and 
advertisements to make products more memorable. It can be used in therapy, evoke emotions, 
improve mood and aid cognitive development.
General Competences:
Learners should acquire the following general Competences:
Analytical thinking: Ability to systematically examine, interpret and evaluate musical elements 
and structures, for purpose of applying them in addressing social-economical needs.
 Creativity and innovation: Ability to experiment new approaches and generate musical ideas 
that exhibit originality   and take creative risks in the composition, performance and interpretation 
of music. 
Critical thinking: Ability to analyse, investigate and evaluate musical works, performances and 
practices with a discerning and reflective mindset to address social-economical needs.  
Collaboration: Ability to work effectively with others in various musical setting such as 
ensembles or bands and other music projects    to achieve goals in Musical Arts Education.
Communication: Ability to express and convey musical ideas appropriately, clearly and 
effectively (verbal and non-verbal).
 Emotional intelligence: Ability to recognise, understand, express and manage emotional stress 
in oneself and others through musical activities.
Problem solving: Ability to use music to identify, analyse and address social-economical 
challenges and obstacles.
TERMINOLOGY
Antecedent: The first part of a melodic phrase that sets up a musical idea, often ending 
in uncertainty, to be resolved by the consequent.
Conjunct motion: Smooth, stepwise movement between, consecutive pitches in
     	
a melody e.g. (C-D-E).
Contour: 	
The overall shape or pattern of ups and downs in pitch, creating a unique
	
outline or topography.
Disjunct motion: Movement between consecutive pitches in a melody by larger 
intervals, often creating a sense of leap or jump, e.g. (C-E, C-G)
Hill: 	
This refers to a musical phrase or section that gradually rise in pitch, often 
creating a sense of tension, anticipation or build up.

Musical Arts Education
Teaching Module
2
 Melody: 	
A succession of pitches in rhythm, heard in sequence, creating a musical 
      	
 line that convey emotion, structure and character. 
 Melodic devices: Techniques used by composers to create interest, variety
       	
and emotional impact in a melody.
 Motif: 	
A short, repeated pattern or idea, often just a few notes, that is used to 
build a melody or tune.
 Motion: 	
The way   pitches move in relation to each other, either smoothly (conjunct)
	
	or by   leaps (disjunct).
 Phrase: 	
A group of notes that form a complete musical thought or idea, similar to
         	
a sentence in language
Valley: 	
refers to a musical phrase or section that descends in pitch, often creating 
a sense of relaxation, resolution, or calmness.
 Wave:  	
A musical phrase or section that moves in a wave like motion.
Sub-Topic: 1.5.1. Melodic Motion
 Introduction 
This subtopic focuses on two types of melodic motion, as well as identifies melodic shape. 
Melodic motion refers to the way pitches in rhythm move in relation to each other within a 
melody, creating a sense of progression and direction. 
There are two types of melodic motion: conjunct motion, which proceeds by step from one scale 
degree to the next (i.e., by the interval of a 2nd), and disjunct motion, which proceeds by leap 
(i.e., by intervals larger than a 2nd)
Specific Competence 1.5.1 Describe the melodic motion.
Suggested Methodology: Demonstration and individual activities …
Learning Environment Setup: Any environment such as, (an outdoor), artificial (music room…) 
and technological (video, YouTube…) 
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials: Piano/keyboard, guitars, visual aids such as 
charts…
LEARNING ACTIVITIES
	
 Activity 1: Identifying conjunct and disjunct motion in a melody.
Task 1:  Learners to sing any familiar melodies (e.g. Row, row, row your boat) and 
identify different intervals between the notes of the melody.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
3
TIP
Engage learners in singing different melodies and identify conjunct and disjunct motion in the 
melodies.
Task 2:  Play some notes like these below on the keyboard and ask learners in groups to identi­
fy them as either conjunct or disjunct motion.
                                         
TIP
Divide learners in groups and ask them to identify different notes played on the keyboard as 
either conjunct or disjunct motion.
DISCUSSION
 
z Learners in groups to analyse the given melodies below and identify conjunct and 
disjunct motion. 
  
 Activity 2: Identifying the shape/contour of the melody. (valleys, waves, hill …)

Musical Arts Education
Teaching Module
4
Task 1: Learners listen or sing simple common melodies then identify what a valley, wave or a 
hill.
TIP
Learners to freely explore and identify the shape/contour of different melodies with clear 
examples of valley, waves and hills.
Examples of a valley contour: 
 
z Twinkle, Little Star. The melody starts high, descends to a low point, and then ascends 
again, creating a valley shape.
Wave Contour: 
 
z London Bridge is Falling Down. This traditional children’s song has a melody that 
oscillates up and down creating a wave-like contour.
 
z The Wheels on the Bus: The melody of this children’s song features a wave like 
pattern, with a repetitive motion that mimics the action of the bus parts. 
Hill Contour:
 
z Happy Birthday to You: The melody starts low, rises to a high point and then descends 
slightly, forming a hill contour.
 
z Jingle Bells: The melody starts low, ascends to high point and then remains relatively 
high, creating a hill shape.
Expected Standard: Melodic motion describe correctly.
ASSESSMENT CRITERIA
	
 
z Provide learners with printed melodies and ask them to identify and label they valleys, 
waves and hills.
 
z Give learners a melody with missing notes and ask them to complete the melody by 
adding notes that create a specific contour.
 
z Play a series of melodies with different contours and ask learners to identify the type 
of contour used in each melody.  
 
z Look at the following melody, taken from the popular song “This is What You Came 
For” by Calvin Harris and Rihanna which uses a mix of conjunct and disjunct melodic 
movement. Study the melody and then answer the questions below as a class exercise.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
5
a.	
In which two bars of the song does the melody move by disjunct movement?
b.	
In which two bars of the song does the melody move by conjunct movement?
c.	
What is the note name of the lowest pitch note in this melody? d) Identify the note 
name of the highest pitch note in this melody?
d.	
Measure the interval   between the lowest and highest note
SUMMARY
This subtopic has highlighted the following:
 
z Melodic motion refers to the movement of pitches in a melody.
 
z There are two main types of melodic motion, and these are conjunct and disjunct 
motion. 
 
z The overall shape or pattern of ups and downs in pitch creating a unique outline or 
topography is referred to as a contour.
 
z There are many types of melodic contours in music apart from valleys, waves and 
hill. 
 
z A valley refers to a musical phrase or section that descends in pitch, often creating a 
sense of relaxation, resolution, or calmness.
 
z A wave is a musical phrase or section that moves in a wave like motion.
 
z A hill refers to a musical phrase or section that gradually rise in pitch, often creating 
a sense of tension, anticipation or build up. 
Sub Topic:1.5.2. Simple Melody Writing
Introduction
The main aim of this sub-topic is to look at how to compose simple melodies using a motif, as 
an antecedent (question) and then provide the consequent (answer) with limited range of notes 
and rhythmic patterns.
Simple melody writing refers to the process or technique of creating a short, coherent, and 
memorable musical idea using pitch and rhythm, to convey emotion and mood.
Specific Competence: 1.5.2.1 Compose simple melodies.
Suggested Methodology: Demonstration, Discussion... 
Learning Environment Setup: Any, such as natural (any outdoor environment…) artificial 
(music room…) and technological (video, online research …)
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials: Piano/keyboard, guitar …
LEARNING ACTIVITIES
Activity 1: Discussing motif in melody writing.

Musical Arts Education
Teaching Module
6
Task 1:  Engage learners in small groups and discuss what a motif is in melody 
writing.
TIP
 
Play or sing a well-known melody that features a motif and ask learners to identify and 
discuss motif in the melody.
DISCUSSION
 
z In smaller groups learners study and identify the motifs below:
TIP
 Highlight major features of the motif for each melody below
 
z Learners give examples of motifs from songs of their choices.
 
z With learners, analyse the motifs given as examples.
Activity 2: Distinguishing various phrases in the melody (antecedent /question, consequent /
answer)
Task 1: 
DISCUSSION

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
7
 
z Ask learners to discuss what a phrase is and how to distinguish it in a melody.
 
z Learners distinguish between an antecedent /question and consequent /answer in a 
given melody.
TIP
: 
Encourage exploration among learners by asking them to distinguish between a question and an 
answer in a melody. This can either be done in groups or individually.
 
z Remind learners that just as they often pause between the different sections in a 
sentence (for example, when you say, “wherever you go, there you are”), a melody 
usually pauses slightly at the end of each phrase.
 
z Emphasis should be made that in vocal music, the musical phrases tend to follow the 
phrases and sentences of the text. 
Examples of Antecedent phrases and consequent phrases
The melody for “Mary Had a Little Lamb” is a well -known example of a simple melody.
The antecedent phrase in the melody is:
“Ma-ry had a lit-tle Lamb”
This phrase sets up the expectation for the consequent phrase which completes the musical 
thought:
“Lit-tle lamb, lit-tle lamb”
In the melody “Mary Had a Little Lamb”, possible motif can be identified as:
“Ma-ry had”
This short melodic idea (3-4 notes) is repeated and varied throughout the melody, making it a 
motif.
Here is a breakdown:
 
z “Mar-ry had” (motif)
 
z “alit-tle lamb” (variation of the motif)

Musical Arts Education
Teaching Module
8
 
z “Lit-tle lamb, lit-tle lamb (repetition and variation of the motif)
This motif is then developed and expanded upon to create the complete melody.
Activity 3: Composing Simple Melodies (At least four bars…)
Task 1:
 
z In smaller groups learners to compose a simple four bar melody.
TIP
 
z Note that when composing a melody, the following points should be considered:  
i.	
Time signature and key signature,
ii.	
On which scale degree and beat of the bar does the melody begin?
iii.	
What rhythmic patterns are to be used?
iv.	
Maintain the motif to be used in the melody, and where possible variety it. 
v.	
Provide a consequent to the antecedent. 
vi.	
Sing or hum while composing the melody to internalize it. 
vii.	
End on the tonic with a long note and approach it in stepwise motion and avoid 
unnecessary wide leaps.
Expected Standard: Simple melodies composed accordingly.
ASSESSMENT CRITERIA
 
 
z In smaller groups, learners to complete provided melodies to ‘four’ bars by writing a 
consequent phrase.
 
z In pairs, learners create and vary a simple melodic motif within four bars.
 
z Individually learners compose a ‘four’ melody in any key of their choice.
 
z Sight sing or sight read the composed melodies
SUMMARY
 
z Simple melody writing (composing) refers to the process or technique of creating 
a short, coherent, and memorable musical idea using pitch and rhythm, to convey 
emotion and mood.
 
z A phrase is a unit of a musical thought that consists of a group of notes that form a 
coherent and meaningful musical idea.
 
z An antecedent phrase is the first phrase in a pair of phrases, typically followed by a 
consequent phrase. 
 
z A melody is made up of phrases, which are usually two or four bars long
 
z When composing simple melodies one should consider all necessary steps.
 
z A motif can be, melodic or rhythmic (or it may contain a combination of the two 
elements) and is considered to be the smallest of the ‘building blocks’ – the recognis­
able, reoccurring themes and phrases – that make up a composition

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
9
1.6 TOPIC: FORM
Introduction
Form in music refers to the overall structure or organization of a piece of music. It encompasses 
the arrangement of musical elements such as melody, harmony, rhythm, and tempo to create a 
cohesive and meaningful whole. Form is very important because it gives music a clear beginning, 
middle, and end, making it easier to follow and understand. Form allows composers to create 
contrast between different sections of music, adding variety and interest. 
General competence(s)
Learners should acquire the following general competences:
Analytical thinking: Ability to systematically examine, interpret and evaluate musical elements 
and structures, for purpose of applying them in addressing social- economical needs.
Critical thinking: Ability to analyse, investigate and evaluate musical works, performances and 
practices with a discerning and reflective mindset to address social-economical needs.
Communication: Ability to express and convey musical ideas appropriately, clearly and 
effectively (verbal and non- verbal)
Collaboration: Ability to work effectively with others in various musical setting such as 
ensembles or bands and other music projects to achieve goals in musical arts Education.
Creativity and innovation: Ability to experiment new approaches and generate musical ideas 
that exhibit originality   and take creative risks in the composition, performance and interpretation 
of music.
TERMINOLOGY
Binary Form: A simple musical structure consisting of two contrasting sections (AB)
Ternary Form: A musical structure consisting of three sections, with the first and third sections 
being similar (ABA)
Theme: A recurring melody or idea
Section: A distinct part of the music, often with its own unique characteristics
Transition: A passage connecting two sections.
Rhythm: This is the arrangement of sounds in time, creating a sense of pulse, meter and groove.
Melody: Is a succession of pitches heard in sequence, creating a musical line.
Sequence: A repetition pattern of pitches at a higher or lower level.
Repetition: Repeating a melodic idea or pattern
Variation: Changing a melodic idea or pattern while maintaining its core identity.

Musical Arts Education
Teaching Module
10
1.6.1 Sub-topic: Binary and ternary
Introduction
Binary form is one of the simplest and most common musical forms, used in a wide range of 
musical styles and genres. It is a fundamental concept in music theory, and understanding binary 
form helps one analyse and appreciate the structure of many musical compositions. Binary form 
consists of two contrasting sections, often labeled as A and B. The A section typically presents a 
theme or melody, while the B section presents a contrasting theme or develops the initial theme. 
The two sections are usually of equal or approximately equal length.
Ternary form on the other hand, is a musical structure consisting of three main sections, typically 
represented as A-B-A. This means the piece begins with an initial theme or section (A), followed 
by a contrasting middle section (B), and concludes with a return to the initial theme or section 
(A). Ternary form provides a sense of balance and symmetry, making it a popular choice for 
composers and songwriters.
Example of Binary form
Ternary Form ABA
Specific competence: 1.6.1.1 Construct melody in binary and ternary forms
Suggested Methodology: Demonstration, group and individual activities…

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
11
LEARNING ACTIVITIES
Learning environment setup: You can teach the learners from any of the 
environments such as natural (any outdoor environment…), artificial (music room…) 
and technological (video…)
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials: Piano/Keyboard, blue tooth speaker, 
projectors, and visual aids such as charts…
Activity 1: Detecting rhythmic and melodic contrast
Task 1: Ask learners to listen, sing or play simple melodies like “Baba Black sheep” 
and identify rhythmic and melodic contrast.  
TIP
Promote analytical thinking by allowing learners to listen, sing or play simple 
melodies and identify rhythmic and melodic contrast.  
Task 2: Play a melody with a contrasting contour or rhythm and ask learners to 
imitate it. Then, ask them to create their own melody.
TIP
Encourage creativity among the learners by asking them to create their own melody 
with a contrasting contour. This can either be written or performed (singing or 
instrument playing) 
Task 3: Provide learners with a score or recording a piece. Ask them to analyse the 
form, identifying areas of contrast and unity. 
TIP
Provide each learner with a score and then allow them to analyse the form of the giv­
en song identifying areas of contrast and unity.
Activity 2: Constructing sections of equal length of binary and ternary structure
Task 1: Ask learners to compose a short piece in binary or ternary form ensuring each 
section A and B or A, B and A have the equal number of measures (4 measures each)
TIP
Put learners in groups so that they work as a team and later on allow them to do the 
work individually. Encourage critical thinking and creativity as they work on this 
activity.
Task 2: Class discussion on the characteristics of binary and ternary forms. Ask 
learners to share their compositions and provide feedback on section length and 
contrast.

Musical Arts Education
Teaching Module
12
TIP
Facilitate a class discussion on the characteristics of binary and ternary forms. Ensure 
that each learner is fully involved in the discussion.
Activity 3: Constructing sections of unequal length in binary and ternary design.
Task 1: Ask learners to compose a binary form piece(AB) with sections of unequal 
length (e.g., 4 measures in A and 6 measures in B) or Ask them to compose a ternary 
form piece (ABA) with sections of unequal length (e.g., 4 measures in A, 6 measures 
in B and 4 measures in A.
TIP
Ensure that the constructed measures are not equal. 
Task 2: Provide learners with a partially completed binary or ternary form piece. 
Ask them to expand one section (e.g., add 4 measures to section A in binary form) or 
expand the middle section (B) in ternary form while maintaining the outer sections’ 
length. 
TIP
Ensure that the suggested sections for binary and ternary forms are expanded as ex­
pected.
Task 3: Give learners a score in binary or ternary form with unequal length. Ask them 
to identify and label the sections.
TIP
Ensure that leaners are able to identify and label the sections. Guide the learners 
throughout the lesson.
Expected standard: Melodies in binary and ternary form constructed correctly
ASSESSMENT CRITERIA
Prepare tasks to be administered during and after learning sessions and give carry 
home tasks.
 
z Form and analysis – Analyse and identify the form of provided melodies 
 
z Written and practical assessment (composing and performing melodies in binary or 
ternary form).
SUMMARY
 This topic has highlighted the following:
 
z Musical form refers to the overall structure or organization of a piece of music.
 
z Binary is a simple musical structure with two sections while ternary is a simple mu­
sical structure with three sections.
 
z Form is shaped by theme, section, transition, development and repetition.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
13
TOPIC: 1.7 MUSICAL TERMS AND SIGNS
Introduction 
Musical terms and signs are specialized words and symbols used in music notation to convey 
information about pitch, rhythm, dynamics, articulation, and other aspects of musical performance. 
Musical terms and signs are important in that they help musicians read music fluently, play 
expressively and perform pieces accurately.
There are so many musical terms and signs used in music, as such this topic will not cover all 
of them. This topic is broken down into two subtopics which are: Italian terms that deal with 
interpreting tempo and dynamics.
General Competences:
Learners should acquire the following general Competences:
Analytical thinking: Ability to systematically examine, interpret and evaluate musical elements 
and structures, for purpose of applying them in addressing social-economical needs.
 Creativity and innovation: Ability to experiment new approaches and generate musical ideas 
that exhibit originality   and take creative risks in the composition, performance and interpretation 
of music. 
Critical thinking: Ability to analyse, investigate and evaluate musical works, performances and 
practices with a discerning and reflective mindset to address social-economical needs.  
Collaboration: Ability to work effectively with others in various musical setting such as 
ensembles or bands and other music projects    to achieve goals in Musical Arts Education.
Communication: Ability to express and convey musical ideas appropriately, clearly and 
effectively (verbal and non-verbal).
 Emotional intelligence: Ability to recognise, understand, express and manage emotional stress 
in oneself and others through musical activities.
Problem solving: Ability to use music to identify, analyse and address social-economic 
challenges and obstacles.
TERMINOLOGY
Below are some of the terminologies Interpreting the Italian terms for tempo:
Accelerando (accel.): Getting quicker
Adagio: Leisurely – usually quite slow
Adagietto: Slow, gentle tempo
Allegretto: Moderate, playful tempo
Allegro: (cheerful) fast
Andante: (easy going) At a walking pace

Musical Arts Education
Teaching Module
14
Andantino: Not as slow as andante
A tempo: Returning to the original tempo after a deviation
Grave: Usually very slow
Larghetto: Slow, lyrical tempo, rather broadly
Largo: Very slow, dignified tempo
Lento: Slow tempo, slower than largo
Moderato: Moderate tempo (moderately)
Presto: Very fast
Ritardando: (rit) holding back
Scherzo:  Fast, lively
Vivace: Full of life, fast, lively tempo.
Sub-Topic: 1.7.1 Italian Terms
Introduction 
Most musical terms are written in Italian Language because of the country’s significant 
contributions   to the development of Western classical music. Italian composers were the first to 
write ‘instruction’ words at certain points in their music. Italian musical terms   have   become 
an integral part of the language of music, providing a universal vocabulary for musicians to 
communicate and interpret music accurately.
The term ‘tempo’ is derived from the Italian language. In Italian, ‘tempo’ means ‘time’ and in 
music, it refers to the speed or rate at which a piece of music is performed.
Specific Competence 1.7.1 .1. Interpret the Italian terms for tempo.
Suggested Methodology: Demonstration, Discussion...
Learning Environment Setup: Any environment such as, (any outdoor), artificial ( music 
room…) and technological ( video, YouTube ) 
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials: Piano/keyboard, guitars, visual aids such as 
charts…
LEARNING ACTIVITIES
 Activity 1: Interpreting the Italian terms for tempo (allegro, presto, lento …)
Task 1: Divide learners in groups and give them a musical score sheet with Italian terms. Ask 
them to interpret as well as demonstrate how the music will be performed. 
TIPS

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
15
 
z Engage learners to perform popular music (songs) and apply different tempo.
 
z Learners to listen to piece of different tempo.
Expected Standard: Italian terms for tempo interpreted correctly.
Specific competence: 1.7.1.2. Interpret the Italian terms for dynamics.
Activity: 2. Interpreting the Italian terms for dynamics piano (p), forte (f)…)
DISCUSSION
 to be done in groups.
Task 1 Divide learners in groups and give them a musical score sheet with Italian terms for 
dynamics. Ask them to interpret as well as demonstrate how the music will be performed.
TIP
 
z Engage learners to interpret the Italian terms for dynamics and help them demonstrate 
how they are applied either by singing or playing instruments as prescribed; (Soft, 
loud…)
Below are some of the commonly used Italian terms for dynamics:
 
z Main dynamic markings:
1. Pianissimo (pp): Very soft
2. Piano (p): Soft
3. Mezzo -piano (mp): Moderately soft
4. Mezzo-forte (mf) moderately   loud
5. Forte (f): Loud
6. Fortissimo: (ff) Very loud
 
z Gradual Dynamic Changes:
1. Crescendo (cresc): Gradually getting louder.
2. Decrescendo (decres: Gradually getting softer.
3. Diminuendo (dim) getting softer.
4. Rinforzando (rfz): Gradually getting louder and stronger
 
z Sudden Dynamic Changes:
1. Sforzando (sfz): Sudden, strong accent.
2. Sforzato (sf): Sudden, strong accent (similar to sforzando).
3. Subito: Sudden change in dynamics (e.g, subito piano for sudden softening.)
Expected Standard: Italian terms for dynamics interpreted correctly.
ASSESSMENT CRITERIA

Musical Arts Education
Teaching Module
16
 Prepare tasks to be administered during and after learning sessions and    give carry home 
tasks.
 
z Sight reading/singing.
 
z Playing instruments.
 
z Listening and analysis. 
 
z Written work.
SUMMARY
This subtopic has highlighted the following:
 
z Musical terms and signs are specialized words and symbols used in music notation to 
convey information.
 
z Italian composers were the first to write ‘instruction’ words to music as such Italian 
musical terms have become an integral part of the language of music.
 
z Tempo refers to the speed or rate at which a piece of music is performed.
 
z In music, dynamics refer to the varying degrees of loudness and softness in a musical 
composition. It describes the contrast between quiet and loud sounds, which adds 
expression, emotion, and depth to the music. By varying dynamics, musicians add 
feeling to their performance, making music engaging and impactful to the listener.
Sub-Topic: 1.7.2. Musical Signs
Introduction 
Musical signs also known as musical notation symbols, are graphical representations used to 
convey musical information. They help musicians read and interpret musical score.
Specific Competence 1.7.2.1. Demonstrate musical signs in music composition
Suggested Methodology: Demonstration, group and individual activities …
Learning Environment Setup: Any environment    such as, (any outdoor), artificial (music 
room…) and technological (video, YouTube) 
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials: Piano/keyboard, guitars, visual aids such as 
charts…

z Sight reading/singing.
 
z Playing instruments.
 
z Listening and analysis. 
 
z Written work (composition)
SUMMARY
 
z The word staccato comes from the Italian word meaning “detached” or “disconnected,” 
which means to play notes short and sharply, with a slight silence between each 
note. It is indicated by a small dot (.) placed either above or below the note head, 
signifying that the note should be played short and detached. 
 
z Legato is an Italian term meaning tied together. Legato is a performance technique 
used in music. It creates a smooth, steady flow between notes. Every single note is 
played to its utmost length and then harmonizes straight into the following note, 
allowing a better crescendo or decrescendo of the phrase.
 
z In music, a repeat sign is symbol that indicate a section of music should be repeated.

Musical Arts Education
Teaching Module
18
TOPIC 1.8 HARMONY
Introduction
In music, Harmony refers to the combination of different musical notes played or sung at the 
same time to produce a pleasing sound. It usually supports the melody (the tune) and adds depth, 
emotion and texture to a piece of music.
Harmony is created when two or more notes sound together, forming chords (like C major or 
G minor), or more complex structures. For instance, if someone sings or plays a melody and 
another person sings or plays a different note that blends well with it, they are singing or playing 
in harmony. So when we think of Harmony, we are thinking of layers of sound. 
It is a fundamental aspect of music, enhancing the emotional impact and aesthetic appeal of a 
composition. 
General Competences:
Analytical thinking: Ability to systematically examine, interpret and evaluate musical elements 
and structures, for the purpose of applying them in social-economic needs.
Collaboration: Ability to work effectively with others in various musical settings such as 
ensembles or bands and other music projects to achieve goals in Musical Arts Education.
Creativity and Innovation: Ability to experiment new approaches and generate musical ideas 
that exhibit originality and take risks in the composition, performance and interpretation of music.
Critical thinking: Ability to analyse, investigate and evaluate musical works, performances and 
practices with a discerning and reflective mindset to address social-economical needs.
Digital Literacy: Ability to effectively use hardware and software to analyse, create, produce, 
and share music.
Problem solving: Ability to use music to identify, analyse and address social-economical 
challenges and obstacles.
TERMINOLOGY
Cadence: A chord progression that signals the end of a phrase or section. (perfect cadence (V-I).
Chord: Is formed when two or more different notes are played or sung together. The most 
common is a Triad, which consists of 3 notes (C-E-G).
Chord progression:  Is a sequence of chords played in successions. (I - IV - V - I)
Consonance: A combination of notes that sounds stable, pleasant or resolved.
Dissonance: A combination of notes that sounds tense or unstable; often used to create drama 
or resolution.
Interval: The distance between two notes. Common intervals include 2nd, 3rd, 5th and Octaves.
Inversion: A chord where the root is not the lowest note. Changes the feel/sound of the chord.
Key: The tonal center or home scale of a piece, which determines which chords are most 
commonly used
Triad: A basic three-note chord made of root, third and fifth. (D minor triad=D-F-A.)
Voicing: Exploring different ways to arrange notes in chords can lead to fuller or more varied 
sound such as using inversions.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
19
Subtopic:1.8.1 Triads
Introduction
A triad is a three-note chord built from a root, third and fifth. It is a group of three notes played 
or sung simultaneously. 
When we refer to the root, third and fifth, we mean that:
 
z The root is a base note (foundation note).
 
z The third is a third interval above the root.
 
z The fifth is a fifth note (a perfect 5th) above the root.
Specific competences: 1.8.1.1 Construct triads
Suggested Methodology: Discussion, Demonstration, individual…
Learning environment setup: Any environment such as, (any outdoor), artificial (music 
room…) and technological (video…).
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials: Piano, keyboard, guitars, charts…
LEARNING ACTIVITIES
 Identifying triads (Root, 3rd and 5th).
Activity 1: Identifying triads (roots, third and fifth).
Task 1: Ask learners to identify and label the triads as root, third and fifth on a staff using the 
tonic triad in C major.
            
TIP
 
z Facilitate learners’ involvement in identifying and labelling the triads as root, third 
and fifth. 
DISCUSSION
 
z Break learners into smaller groups to discuss and identify the triads as root, third 
and fifth.

Musical Arts Education
Teaching Module
20
Task 2: Ask learners to either sing or play the constructed tonic triad in C major. 
TIP
Allow learners in groups to freely explore and experiment by either singing or playing the tonic 
triad in C major.  
Activity 2: Constructing triads (root, 3rd, 5th)
Task 1: Engage learners in constructing triads (root, 3rd, 5th) on the staff in different keys.
TIP
 
z Engage and support learners in constructing triads (root, 5th, 3rd) on either treble or 
bass staff. 
Expected standard: Triads constructed correctly.
ASSESSMENT CRITERIA
Prepare tasks to be administered during and after learning sessions and give carry home tasks.
 
z Identify, construct and play/sing the triads given.
 
z Create a quiz to test learners’ ability to identify triads.
 
z Assign a project where learners analyse and identify triads in selected score.
 
z Give short answer questions by asking students to explain triad concepts and give 
musical examples.
 
z Ask learners to compose short pieces of music using triads.
Activity 3: Describing triads (major, minor, diminished or augmented). 
Task 1: Ask learners to describe triads as (major, minor, diminished or augmented).
TIP
 
z Introduce the theoretical and practical application of triads as (major, minor, dimin­
ished or augmented).
 
z Introduce the basics of triads-a triad is a three-note chord built from a root, 3rd and 
5th.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
21
 
z Guide learners how to identify intervals in a triad e.g. Major 3rd which has 3 steps 
with 4 semitones – (C to E) and minor 3rd has 3 steps with 3 semitones (C to E 
flat…)
 
z Major triad –structure is root +major third +perfect fifth
 
z Minor triad structure has root + minor third + perfect.
Activity 4: Inverting triads.
Teaching inverting triads involves a structured approach that helps learners understand the con­
cept of triads, their inversions and how to identify and perform them.
Task 1: Invert triads from root position to first and second inversion.
TIPS
 
z Guide learners on how to invert triads from root, first and second inversion.
 
z Provide worksheets where learners must label triads and their inversions in musical 
notation.
 
z Have learners practice performing triads and their inversions on their instruments.
 
z Start with simple major and minor triads before moving to diminished and augmented.
Activity 5: Playing triads.
Task 1: Perform each type of triad (Major, minor, diminished and augmented) on an instrument 
(piano, guitar…)
TIPS
 
z Guide learners how to play triads on an instrument
 
z Give learners a root note and have them play the third and the fifth notes.
 
z Engage learners in ear training by identifying the played triads (Major, minor...)
 
z Comparative listening-guide: learners to play in pairs e.g. major vs minor and have 
students describe emotional quality of each.
Specific competence: 1.8.1.2 Apply primary and secondary triads
Suggested Methodology: Discussion, Demonstration, individual…
Learning environment setup: Any environment such as, (any outdoor), artificial (music 
room…) and technological (video…).
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials: Piano, keyboard, guitars, charts…
LEARNING ACTIVITIES
Activity 1: Identifying primary and secondary triads in any given scale.

Musical Arts Education
Teaching Module
22
Task 1: Learners to identify primary and secondary triads in groups in any given scale.
TIP
 Carefully guide learners how to identify primary and secondary triads in any given scale.
Activity 2: Applying primary and secondary triads in any given scale.
Task 1: Learners construct primary and secondary triads in any given scale/key.
TIP
 
z Guide learners how to construct primary and secondary triads in any given key/
scale.
Expected standard: Primary and secondary triads in any given scale applied correctly.
ASSESSMENT CRITERIA
Prepare tasks to be administered during and after learning sessions and give carry home tasks.
 
z Create a quiz to test learners’ ability to understand primary and secondary triads in 
any given key/scale.
 
z Assign a project where learners analyse and identify primary and secondary triads in 
any given key/scale.
 
z Ask learners to create and notate simple chord progressions of primary and secondary 
triads in any given key/scale.
SUMMARY
 This topic has highlighted the following:
 
z Harmony refers to the combination of different musical notes played or sung at the 
same time to produce a pleasing sound.
 
z A triad is a three-note chord built from a root, third and fifth. It is a group of three 
notes played or sung simultaneously. 
      When we refer to the root, third and fifth, we mean that:
 
z The root is a base note (foundation note).
 
z The third is a third interval above the root.
 
z The fifth is a fifth note (a perfect 5th) above the root.

SUBJECT: MUSICAL ARTS EDUCATION	
	
	
	
TOPIC: 1.8 HARMONY   	
SUB-TOPIC: TRIADS	
GENERAL COMPETENCES: Analytical thinking, Collaboration, Creativity and Innovation, 
Critical thinking, Digital thinking and Problem solving.
SPECIFIC COMPETENCES: 1.8.1.1 Construct triads
RATIONALE: This lesson will focus on constructing triads which is a fundamental skill in 
music theory, designed to help learners recognise and appreciate harmony in music. In this 
lesson, learners will acquire a skill in constructing triads through demonstration, discussion, and 
group work and think pair. Understanding how to build triads, empowers learners to create their 
own music and apply harmony in a real life situation. This lesson is lesson 1 in a series of 7.
PRIOR KNOWLEDGE: Pitch, Intervals and Melody (basic music notation, scale knowledge 
staff notation, note reading, note names and positions, basic chord concept, note values and rests)
REFERENCES: 
 
z 2025 Musical Arts Education Form 1- Teaching Module page 33.
 
z Harmony theory- James e. Perone page 55.
 
z WWW.youtube video on Triads.

Natural environment: (any outdoor)
Artificial environment/ Technological environment: (music room…) and technological 
(video…)

charts…

completion of all the lined up activities)

29
LESSON EVALUATION:
Learners were able to construct triads in root, 3rd and 5th positions by applying critical and 
analytical thinking in identifying different triads. However, five learners experienced difficulty 
in reading staff notation which slowed down triad construction. There is need to revisit basic note 
reading and intervals in the next lesson to reinforce foundation.
